slots.text: Most founders skip user interviews because they're afraid their idea sucks. The irony is that's exactly when you need them most.

Signs your validation is actually working:
1. Users describe the problem in their own words
2. They ask when it'll be ready
3. They mention budget unprompted
4. They introduce you to their team
The hardest part about building with AI isn't the technical stuff. It's figuring out which parts of your workflow actually benefit from being automated vs which parts just feel like they should be.
Founders who talk to 5 users build features. Founders who talk to 50 users build products. The difference is pattern recognition.
Every workflow has exactly one step that makes people want to quit. Find it, fix it, win.
User feedback falls into two buckets: what they say they want and what they actually pay for. The gap between these is where most startups die.
Most founders skip validation because they're afraid of hearing 'no.' But 'no' with specifics is worth 10x more than 'yes' without context.

3 signs your user interviews are garbage:

1. People only say nice things
2. You're explaining your idea more than listening
3. Every conversation ends with 'sounds interesting'

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the tech. It's figuring out which manual processes are actually worth automating. Most workflows people complain about exist for good reasons they forgot.
Founders who talk to 5 users think they found product-market fit. Founders who talk to 50 users realize they know nothing. There's no middle ground.
Removing friction sounds good until you realize some friction is intentional. Speed bumps exist because someone learned something expensive.

4 questions that separate real problems from complaints:

1. What workaround do you use now?
2. How much time does this waste weekly?
3. Who else feels this pain?
4. What budget exists to fix it?
Most idea validation fails because founders ask what people want instead of watching what they actually do. Behavior trumps opinions every time.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions that confirm your bias
2. Talking to people who aren't actually your users
3. Asking about hypotheticals instead of past behavior
The AI tooling paradox: we can build features faster than ever, but we're also building the wrong things faster than ever. Speed without direction is just expensive wandering.
Noticed that founders who spend weeks perfecting their workflow automation usually have the messiest actual processes. The tools become procrastination dressed up as productivity.
User feedback is a lagging indicator. By the time they tell you something's broken, they've already mentally moved on to your competitor.

Signs your workflow has too much friction:

1. You avoid doing tasks you know you should do
2. Simple things require opening multiple tools
3. You're spending more time managing the process than executing
4. New team members take forever to get productive
Most founders skip validation because they think they already know their users. Then they build for 6 months and realize they were solving the wrong problem entirely.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions that get you the answers you want
2. Talking more than listening
3. Taking feedback from people who will never pay you
The founders who succeed with AI tools aren't the ones chasing every new model release. They're the ones who found one boring use case and automated it completely.
Workflow friction is invisible until you map it out step by step. That 'quick' task your team does 20 times a day? It's probably eating 2 hours and involves 4 different tools for no good reason.
Stop asking users what features they want. Start watching what they actually do when they think no one is looking.

Signs your idea validation is working:

1. Users describe the problem before you mention it
2. They ask when they can start paying
3. They offer to introduce you to others with the same problem
4. They get frustrated when your demo ends
Most founders skip talking to users because they're scared their idea sucks. The faster you find out it sucks, the faster you can build something that doesn't.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions that validate what you want to hear
2. Talking to friends who lie to be nice
3. Asking what people want instead of what they actually do
The founders who validate ideas fastest are the ones comfortable with hearing 'no' 50 times in a row. Everyone else gets stuck polishing concepts that will never work.
AI tools promise to automate everything but most just add another step to your workflow. You still need to prompt, review, edit, format. Sometimes the 'manual' way is actually faster.
Removing friction isn't about fewer clicks. It's about fewer decisions. Every choice you force users to make is where they bail.

Signs your workflow has too much friction:

1. You avoid using your own product
2. Users ask for features that already exist
3. Support tickets about 'how do I...'
4. High signup, low activation
Most founders skip validation because they're afraid of hearing 'no.' But getting 100 nos fast is infinitely better than building for 6 months into a maybe.

User interviews that actually matter:

1. Ask about their current broken process
2. Listen for words they repeat
3. Ask for introductions to similar people
4. Skip leading questions entirely
5. End with 'what didn't I ask?'
AI tools promise to remove friction but often just move it around. Now instead of wrestling with Excel, you're wrestling with prompts. The friction isn't gone—it's just wearing a different disguise.
Founders who talk to 10 users in week 1 move faster than founders who talk to 50 users in month 3. Speed of feedback loops beats sample size every time.
The best workflow improvements feel invisible. You don't notice them working—you just notice you stopped complaining about that thing.
Everyone says build an MVP but no one defines 'minimum.' Minimum for learning isn't minimum for selling. Most founders confuse the two and end up with something that does neither well.
Most founders skip validation because they think their idea is too obvious to need it. The obvious ideas are exactly the ones that need the most validation.

Three things that kill user interviews:

1. Leading questions that confirm your bias
2. Talking to friends who want to be nice
3. Asking what they would do instead of what they actually do
Building with AI feels like having a really smart intern who sometimes just makes things up. You get 80% there fast, then spend forever debugging the last 20% because you can't tell what's real.
The founders who remove the most friction from their workflows are usually the ones adding the most friction to their users' workflows.
User feedback falls into two buckets: what they say they want and what they actually pay for. The gap between these is where most startups die.

Signs your validation is working:

1. People interrupt you to ask follow-up questions
2. They offer to pay before you mention price
3. They introduce you to others with the same problem
4. They ask when it's ready
